content: Patient safety encompasses prevention of healthcare-associated harm. Key categories: (1) Medication errors - wrong drug, dose, patient, or route (affecting 5% of hospitalized patients); (2) Hospital-acquired infections - CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, C. difficile (7% of patients in developed countries); (3) Surgical complications - wrong-site surgery, retained instruments, postoperative sepsis; (4) Diagnostic errors - delayed/missed diagnoses (estimated 10-15% of cases); (5) Healthcare-associated venous thromboembolism. The WHO estimates 134 million adverse events annually in low/middle-income countries causing 2.6 million deaths. In the US, preventable harm causes ~250,000 deaths/year, making it the third leading cause of death.

Evidence-based safety interventions include: (1) Checklists - WHO Surgical Safety Checklist reduced complications by 36%, mortality by 47%; (2) Hand hygiene programs - multimodal strategies (education, reminders, feedback) reduce HAIs by 30-50%; (3) Medication reconciliation - pharmacist-led review at transitions reduces adverse drug events by 50%; (4) Central line bundles - maximal barrier precautions, chlorhexidine skin prep, daily line necessity review reduced CLABSI by >70%; (5) Early warning scores - MEWS/NEWS triggering rapid response teams reduce cardiac arrests by 20-30%; (6) Culture of safety - non-punitive error reporting, leadership commitment, teamwork training. CMS no longer pays for 'never events' (wrong-site surgery, retained objects) since 2008.

Patient safety measurement uses: (1) AHRQ Patient Safety Indicators (PSIs) - 21 measures derived from administrative data (e.g., PSI-9 postoperative hemorrhage, PSI-12 DVT/PE); (2) CDC NHSN surveillance - standardized infection ratios for HAIs comparing observed to predicted infections; (3) Trigger tools - retrospective chart review using 'triggers' (antidote administration, lab abnormalities) identifying adverse events missed by voluntary reporting; (4) Global Trigger Tool - IHI method finding 30-50% of patients experience harm; (5) Patient-reported safety concerns. Hospital-acquired condition rates: CLABSI 0.5-2.0 per 1,000 line-days, CAUTI 1.5-4.0 per 1,000 catheter-days, C. difficile 3-8 per 10,000 patient-days. Top decile hospitals achieve 50% lower rates than bottom decile.
